exercises.title: Video is not a stack of photos
exercises.prompt: Explain why video requires more than image understanding (causal ordering, action verbs, event timing) and why temporal positional encoding changes video-VLM performance independently of the vision encoder.
exercises.hint: A 5s clip has order, motion, and timing an image model can't represent. Temporal position encoding tells the model WHEN each frame is, so it can reason about order/timing — independent of how good the per-frame encoder is.
exercises.solution:
**Why video needs more than image understanding:** an image is a single instant; a video has **temporal structure** an image model fundamentally cannot represent:
- **Causal ordering:** 'the glass fell *then* shattered' vs 'shattered *then* fell' — the **order of frames** carries meaning. An image model sees frames as an unordered set.
- **Action verbs / motion:** 'opening' vs 'closing' a door, 'walking toward' vs 'away' — these are defined by **change across frames** (motion), invisible in any single frame.
- **Event timing:** 'what happened at 0:03', 'how long did X take', 'what came after Y' — requires knowing **when** each frame occurred. Temporal grounding (localizing events in time) is a core video task.
Treating video as a bag of frames loses all of this — you can recognize objects per frame but not reason about **what happens, in what order, when**.

**Why temporal positional encoding changes performance independently of the encoder:** the **vision encoder** determines how well the model perceives **each frame** (objects, scene, text). But even a perfect per-frame encoder leaves the model unable to reason about **time** unless it knows **the temporal position of each frame**. **Temporal positional encoding** (e.g. M-RoPE's time axis, TMRoPE, absolute timestamps) tells the model **when each frame is in the sequence** — its order and (with absolute time) its actual timestamp. This is a **separate axis from perception**: 
- A better encoder → better **per-frame understanding** (what's in each frame).
- Better temporal positions → better **cross-frame temporal reasoning** (order, timing, duration, motion).
You can improve one without the other, and **temporal grounding accuracy depends heavily on the temporal encoding** even when the encoder is fixed. Empirically, swapping in a proper temporal positional scheme (like Qwen2.5-VL's TMRoPE) **closes the gap with frontier video models** without changing the encoder — proving temporal encoding is an **independent, first-class lever** for video VLMs. The two axes — **perceive each frame (encoder)** and **place frames in time (temporal positions)** — are both required, and improving the temporal one is often the higher-leverage fix for video-specific tasks (timing, ordering, grounding).

exercises.title: Frame sampling and temporal token designs
exercises.prompt: Implement and compare uniform, dynamic-FPS, and event-driven frame sampling on tokens-per-second vs grounding accuracy. Then compare the three temporal-token designs: Q-former-per-clip (Video-LLaMA), pooled-per-frame (Video-LLaVA), M-RoPE-per-token (Qwen2.5-VL).
exercises.hint: Uniform: fixed FPS (simple, misses bursts). Dynamic-FPS: more frames where motion is dense. Event-driven: sample at scene/event changes. Designs: Q-former compresses each clip to K tokens; pooled-per-frame pools each frame to few tokens; M-RoPE keeps per-token with 3-axis positions.
exercises.solution:
```python
def uniform_sample(video, fps=1):
    return [video.frame(t) for t in range(0, video.duration, 1//fps if fps>=1 else 1)]

def dynamic_fps_sample(video, motion, base=1, peak=8):
    frames = []
    for sec, m in enumerate(motion):
        n = base + int((peak - base) * m)           # more frames where motion is high
        frames += [video.frame(sec + i/n) for i in range(n)]
    return frames

def event_driven_sample(video, scene_changes):
    return [video.frame(t) for t in scene_changes]  # sample at event boundaries
```

**Frame-sampling comparison (tokens/sec vs grounding accuracy):**
- **Uniform (fixed FPS):** sample every 1/FPS seconds regardless of content. **Simple and predictable token cost**, but **wastes tokens on static stretches** and **misses brief events between samples** — poor for precise temporal grounding (a 1-FPS sample misses a 200ms action). Constant tokens/sec, mediocre event detection.
- **Dynamic-FPS:** sample **more frames where motion/events are dense, fewer when static** (Lesson 9). **Better grounding accuracy per token** (spends the budget where events are) — but variable token cost and needs motion scoring. **Requires absolute-time alignment** so the model knows each frame's real timestamp despite variable sampling.
- **Event-driven:** sample at **scene/event boundaries** (shot changes, action onsets). **Most token-efficient** for content-aligned coverage and good at catching distinct events — but may **miss continuous motion within an event** and depends on good boundary detection.
The tradeoff is always **tokens/sec (cost) vs temporal resolution (grounding accuracy)** — uniform is cheap but blind to event density; dynamic/event-driven spend the budget intelligently at the cost of complexity. (All bounded by the visual-token budget, Lessons 1, 8.)

**Three temporal-token designs:**
- **Q-former-per-clip (Video-LLaMA):** split the video into clips, run a **Q-Former per clip** to compress each into **K fixed tokens** (Lesson 3's fixed-budget bridge applied per clip). *Tradeoff:* **strong compression** (constant tokens per clip, good for long video), but the **bottleneck loses per-frame detail** and clip boundaries can fragment events.
- **Pooled-per-frame (Video-LLaVA):** encode each frame and **pool it down to a few tokens**, concatenate across frames. *Tradeoff:* **simple, preserves per-frame structure** at low detail, scales with frame count (can explode for long video). The LLaVA-style approach extended to video.
- **M-RoPE-per-token (Qwen2.5-VL / TMRoPE):** keep **per-token representations** with **multi-axis positions (time, height, width)** so each visual token knows its 2D position *and* its timestamp. *Tradeoff:* **richest temporal+spatial reasoning** (no compression bottleneck, precise per-token time/space), best grounding — but **most tokens** (no per-clip/frame compression), so it needs smart frame sampling to control cost.
The progression mirrors the field: **heavy compression (Q-former) → light pooling (per-frame) → full per-token with rich positions (M-RoPE)** — trading token budget for temporal-reasoning fidelity, with M-RoPE+dynamic sampling now dominant because precise per-token time/space positions give the best grounding when you can afford (or smartly budget) the tokens.

exercises.title: Evaluating temporal grounding and the video-VLM design space
exercises.prompt: Design a temporal-grounding evaluation and explain the four video benchmarks (VideoMME, TempCompass, EgoSchema, Video-MMMU), what each measures, the failure modes of video VLMs, and how the token-budget vs temporal-resolution tension shapes the whole video-VLM design space (pointing to long-video, Lesson 18).
exercises.hint: Temporal grounding: localize an event in time (which second). Benchmarks: VideoMME (general), TempCompass (temporal reasoning), EgoSchema (long egocentric), Video-MMMU (reasoning). Failures: temporal hallucination, frame sampling misses, lost timing. Tension: more frames = better timing but more tokens → long video breaks the budget (Lesson 18).
exercises.solution:
**Designing a temporal-grounding eval:** temporal grounding = **localize an event in time** ('at what second does the person pick up the cup?'). To evaluate:
1. Build a dataset of **(video, question, ground-truth time/interval)** examples covering events at **various timestamps** (including subtle/brief ones).
2. Ask the model to **predict the time/interval** of the queried event.
3. Score with **temporal IoU** (overlap of predicted vs true interval) or **accuracy within a tolerance** (e.g. ±1s), and measure **recall at specific timestamps** (does it find events at minute X?).
4. Vary **frame sampling** (uniform/dynamic/event-driven) and **temporal encoding** to isolate their effect on grounding accuracy — and test events between sampled frames (to expose sampling blind spots).

**The four video benchmarks:**
- **VideoMME:** **general video understanding** across diverse domains and video lengths — a broad multi-task video QA benchmark (the 'MMLU of video').
- **TempCompass:** specifically targets **temporal reasoning** — order, direction, speed, attribute change over time — designed to catch models that understand frames but **not time** (a model can pass image-style VQA but fail TempCompass).
- **EgoSchema:** **long-form egocentric (first-person) video** understanding — tests reasoning over **long temporal context** (minutes of first-person video), stressing long-video capability.
- **Video-MMMU:** **reasoning-heavy** video understanding (expert/academic content) — combines perception with multi-step reasoning over video.
Together they cover **general understanding (VideoMME), temporal reasoning (TempCompass), long-form (EgoSchema), and reasoning (Video-MMMU)** — a model must handle all to be a strong video VLM.

**Failure modes of video VLMs:**
- **Temporal hallucination:** describing events in the **wrong order** or **inventing timing** — the model perceives frames but reasons about time incorrectly (weak temporal encoding).
- **Frame-sampling misses:** the queried event **fell between sampled frames** (uniform sampling at low FPS) so the model never saw it → confidently wrong.
- **Lost timing / order:** without good temporal positions, the model **collapses the video into a bag of frames** and can't answer order/duration questions.
- **Long-video degradation:** performance **drops sharply as video length grows** (too many frames for the budget → aggressive pooling loses detail, or events are missed) — the long-video problem.
- **Static bias:** answering from a single representative frame (image-style) and ignoring motion.

**How the token-budget vs temporal-resolution tension shapes the design space:** the core video-VLM constraint is **frames × tokens-per-frame = budget** (Lessons 1, 8). Every design decision trades **temporal coverage/resolution against token cost**:
- **More frames / higher FPS** → better **event timing and grounding** (catch brief events, fine motion) but **more tokens** (quadratic attention, context limits, cost).
- **Fewer frames / heavier pooling** → **cheaper** but **misses events and loses temporal detail** (worse grounding).
This tension drives **everything**: frame-sampling strategies (uniform→dynamic→event-driven, to spend the budget where events are), temporal-token designs (Q-former compression → per-frame pooling → per-token M-RoPE, trading compression for fidelity), and temporal encoding (to extract maximal temporal reasoning per token). For **short clips**, smart sampling + a fixed budget (LLaVA-OneVision, Lesson 8) suffices. But the tension becomes **unmanageable for long video**: a 30-minute or hour-long video has **far too many frames** — even at a few tokens each, you **blow past any fixed budget**, and pooling aggressively enough to fit **destroys the detail and timing** needed for grounding. There's no sampling/pooling that gives both **long temporal coverage AND fine grounding** within a fixed budget. **This is exactly the problem Lesson 18 (long-video, million-token) tackles** — when smart budgeting isn't enough, you need fundamentally different scaling paths: **brute-force long context** (Gemini 1.5's 10M tokens), **ring attention** (LWM), **token compression** (LongVILA/Video-XL), or **agentic retrieval** (VideoAgent — retrieve relevant moments instead of ingesting everything). So this lesson's tension (frames vs tokens, coverage vs grounding) is the **direct setup** for long-video: video VLMs work well at short lengths via smart sampling and temporal encoding, but **long video breaks the budget**, forcing the architectural innovations of the next lesson.
